subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 16, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Characterizing Infections in Children after Out-of-Hospital Ca
sentences: Story 1 is about Characterizing Infections in Children after Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest..
This is a recent research article from Resuscitation.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in children < 18 years old admitted to the PICU within 6 hours after OHCA between 2017-2022, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Of 203 included children, 40 (20%) had a "definite or probable" bacterial infection.

Children with definite or probable bacterial infections had longer median CPR duration (24 versus 10 minutes, p<0.001) and lower rates of survival to hospital discharge (33% versus 70%, p<0.01) compared to those with possible or no bacterial infection.

In a multivariable logistic regression model adjusted for age, CPR duration and baseline respiratory support, definite or probable bacterial infection was associated with lower odds of survival to hospital discharge (aOR 0.39, 95% CI 0.15-1.00, P=0.05) and ICU discharge (aOR 0.38, 95% CI 0.15-0.98, P=0.046), though the upper bound of the 95% confidence interval crossed 1 for some analyses.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Non-invasive removal of the Smart tracheal occlusion device fo
sentences: Story 2 is about Non-invasive removal of the Smart tracheal occlusion device for fetal congenital diaphragmatic hernia: a single-arm, open-label, phase 1 study..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from Lancet (London, England).

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patient age 18 years or more, singleton pregnancy with CDH, and severe or moderate pulmonary hypoplasia in left-sided CDH; in Leuven, eligibility criteria also included severe hypoplasia, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Between Aug 4, 2021 and Jan 15, 2024, 48 patients were enrolled.
Of these, 47 patients underwent FETO, and the Smart-TO was successfully placed in 46 fetuses.
The deflation rate was 100% (95% CI 92-100).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Positive Pleural Fluid Cultures in the ICU: A 10-Year Populati
sentences: Story 3 is about Positive Pleural Fluid Cultures in the ICU: A 10-Year Population-Based Cohort Study of Prevalence, Microbial Characteristics, and Clinical Associations..
This is a cohort study from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in ICU, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
A total of 1251 patients were included, of whom 51 (4.1%) had a positive first pleural culture.
Suspected pleural infection was associated with a higher positive culture risk (adjusted risk ratio [RR]: 4.88; 95% CI, 2.70-8.83).
No such association was observed for parapneumonic effusions (adjusted RR: 1.41; 95% CI, 0.83-2.38).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Useful for risk stratification and hypothesis generation, but avoid causal bedside changes unless supported by interventional evidence.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Characterizing Infections in Children after Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest.
articles.summary: Single-center, retrospective cohort study including children < 18 years old admitted to the PICU within 6 hours after OHCA between 2017-2022. Children were classified as having "definite or probable" or "possible or no" bacterial infections recognized within 48 hours of admission using standardized criteria. Of 203 included children, 40 (20%) had a "definite or probable" bacterial infection. Children with definite or probable bacterial infections had longer median CPR duration (24 versus 10 minutes, p<0.001) and lower rates of survival to hospital discharge (33% versus 70%, p<0.01) compared to those with possible or no bacterial infection. In a multivariable logistic regression model adjusted for age, CPR duration and baseline respiratory support, definite or probable bacterial infection was associated with lower odds of survival to hospital discharge (aOR 0.39, 95% CI 0.15-1.00, P=0.05) and ICU discharge (aOR 0.38, 95% CI 0.15-0.98, P=0.046), though the upper bound of the 95% confidence interval crossed 1 for some analyses.
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Of 203 included children, 40 (20%) had a "definite or probable" bacterial infection. Children with definite or probable bacterial infections had longer median CPR duration (24 versus 10 minutes, p<0.001) and lower rates of survival to hospital discharge (33% versus 70%, p<0.01) compared to those with possible or no bacterial infection. In a multivariable...

keyResults: Of 203 included children, 40 (20%) had a "definite or probable" bacterial infection.

Children with definite or probable bacterial infections had longer median CPR duration (24 versus 10 minutes, p<0.001) and lower rates of survival to hospital discharge (33% versus 70%, p<0.01) compared to those with possible or no bacterial infection.

In a multivariable logistic regression model adjusted for age, CPR duration and baseline respiratory support, definite or probable bacterial infection was associated with lower odds of survival to hospital discharge (aOR 0.39, 95% CI 0.15-1.00, P=0.05) and ICU discharge (aOR 0.38, 95% CI 0.15-0.98, P=0.046), though the upper bound of the 95% confidence interval crossed 1 for some analyses.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

The most clinically relevant signal was Of 203 included children, 40 (20%) had a "definite or probable" bacterial infection..
The confidence interval suggests how much uncertainty remains around the effect estimate.
For bedside practice, this matters because Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically..
I would interpret this cautiously because Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix..

englishNotes.speakingPrompt: In one sentence, explain whether Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. should change ICU practice today.

articles.title: Non-invasive removal of the Smart tracheal occlusion device for fetal congenital diaphragmatic hernia: a single-arm, open-label, phase 1 study.
articles.summary: Eligibility criteria included patient age 18 years or more, singleton pregnancy with CDH, and severe or moderate pulmonary hypoplasia in left-sided CDH; in Leuven, eligibility criteria also included severe hypoplasia in right-sided and bilateral CDH. Balloon deflation was scheduled between 34+0 weeks and 34+6 weeks (or earlier, if clinically indicated) by exposure to the magnetic field of a MRI machine, and ultrasonography was then done to confirm balloon deflation. The primary outcome was reported with its 95% CI using the continuity-corrected score method for all patients with the investigational medical device in place at the time of deflation. Between Aug 4, 2021 and Jan 15, 2024, 48 patients were enrolled. Of these, 47 patients underwent FETO, and the Smart-TO was successfully placed in 46 fetuses.
articles.analysis: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.

pico.population: patient age 18 years or more, singleton pregnancy with CDH, and severe or moderate pulmonary hypoplasia in left-sided CDH; in Leuven, eligibility criteria also included severe hypoplasia
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Main outcomes are those reported in the abstract; prioritize patient-centered outcomes such as mortality, ventilation duration, ICU stay, and adverse events.

keyResults: Between Aug 4, 2021 and Jan 15, 2024, 48 patients were enrolled.
Of these, 47 patients underwent FETO, and the Smart-TO was successfully placed in 46 fetuses.
Median gestational age at FETO was 29+2 weeks (28+5-30+1 weeks).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.
articles.practiceImpact: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.

The confidence interval suggests how much uncertainty remains around the effect estimate.
For bedside practice, this matters because Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions..
I would interpret this cautiously because Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix..

articles.title: Positive Pleural Fluid Cultures in the ICU: A 10-Year Population-Based Cohort Study of Prevalence, Microbial Characteristics, and Clinical Associations.
articles.summary: A total of 1251 patients were included, of whom 51 (4.1%) had a positive first pleural culture. Suspected pleural infection was associated with a higher positive culture risk (adjusted risk ratio [RR]: 4.88; 95% CI, 2.70-8.83). No such association was observed for parapneumonic effusions (adjusted RR: 1.41; 95% CI, 0.83-2.38). A positive culture was associated with increased 90-day mortality (adjusted RR: 1.35; 95% CI, 1.04-1.73). Pleural effusions are common in the ICU, often requiring drainage.
articles.analysis: Useful for risk stratification and hypothesis generation, but avoid causal bedside changes unless supported by interventional evidence.

pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: A total of 1251 patients were included, of whom 51 (4.1%) had a positive first pleural culture. Suspected pleural infection was associated with a higher positive culture risk (adjusted risk ratio [RR]: 4.88; 95% CI, 2.70-8.83). No such association was observed for parapneumonic effusions (adjusted RR: 1.41; 95% CI, 0.83-2.38). A positive culture was...

keyResults: A total of 1251 patients were included, of whom 51 (4.1%) had a positive first pleural culture.
Suspected pleural infection was associated with a higher positive culture risk (adjusted risk ratio [RR]: 4.88; 95% CI, 2.70-8.83).
No such association was observed for parapneumonic effusions (adjusted RR: 1.41; 95% CI, 0.83-2.38).
A positive culture was associated with increased 90-day mortality (adjusted RR: 1.35; 95% CI, 1.04-1.73).
criticalAppraisal: Observational evidence: useful for association and prognosis, but residual confounding is likely.
Check confounding by indication, adjustment variables, immortal-time bias, and missing outcome data.
articles.practiceImpact: Useful for risk stratification and hypothesis generation, but avoid causal bedside changes unless supported by interventional evidence.

For bedside practice, this matters because Useful for risk stratification and hypothesis generation, but avoid causal bedside changes unless supported by interventional evidence..
I would interpret this cautiously because Check confounding by indication, adjustment variables, immortal-time bias, and missing outcome data..
